stem: A 28-year-old medical intern sustains a hollow-bore needle-stick injury to her right index finger while taking blood from a patient known to be HIV-positive with a detectable viral load of 45,000 copies per mL. She washes the wound immediately with soap and water and presents to the emergency department within 30 minutes. She has no allergies, is not pregnant, takes no regular medications, and has normal renal and hepatic function. Baseline HIV serology is negative.

data_table.Investigations: Baseline HIV Ag/Ab: negative. Hepatitis B sAg: negative, sAb > 100 IU/L. Hepatitis C Ab: negative. FBC, UEC, LFTs: normal. Pregnancy test: negative.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis regimen?

options.text: Zidovudine plus lamivudine twice daily for a total of 28 days
options.rationale: Zidovudine-based dual therapy was the original PEP regimen but is no longer recommended as first-line due to higher adverse effect burden and inferior tolerability compared to tenofovir-based triple therapy. It would only be considered if tenofovir were contraindicated. (Source: ASHM PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Tenofovir plus emtricitabine daily for a total of 14 days only
options.rationale: The correct backbone is tenofovir plus emtricitabine, but 14 days is insufficient. All current guidelines recommend a full 28-day course of triple-drug PEP to maximise efficacy. Shortened courses have not been validated. (Source: ASHM PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Dolutegravir as single-agent therapy daily for a total of 28 days
options.rationale: Dolutegravir monotherapy is not a recommended PEP regimen. PEP requires at least a three-drug combination to provide adequate viral suppression. Monotherapy risks incomplete prophylaxis and potential resistance. (Source: ASHM PEP Guidelines)

options.text: Tenofovir plus emtricitabine with dolutegravir daily for 28 days
options.rationale: Current Australian guidelines recommend tenofovir disoproxil 300 mg plus emtricitabine 200 mg daily combined with dolutegravir 50 mg daily for 28 days, initiated ideally within 72 hours of exposure. This regimen has superior tolerability and completion rates compared to older regimens. (Source: ASHM PEP Guidelines; Australasian Society for HIV, Viral Hepatitis and Sexual Health Medicine)

options.text: Observation alone with repeat HIV serology at 6 weeks and 12 weeks
options.rationale: Observation without PEP is inappropriate for a significant percutaneous exposure from a source with detectable viraemia. PEP is strongly indicated when the source is HIV-positive and the exposure involves a hollow-bore needle. Seroconversion monitoring alone would miss the prophylaxis window. (Source: ASHM PEP Guidelines)

explanation.summary: HIV post-exposure prophylaxis should be commenced as soon as possible after a significant occupational exposure, ideally within 72 hours. The current Australian standard is a 28-day course of tenofovir-emtricitabine plus dolutegravir. Risk assessment considers the type of exposure, source viral load, and device involved. Follow-up serology is performed at 6 weeks, 3 months, and 6 months post-exposure.
key_points: PEP must begin within 72 hours of exposure for efficacy
Three-drug regimen is standard for all significant exposures
Hollow-bore needle from viraemic source is highest risk percutaneous exposure
Baseline testing includes HIV, hepatitis B and C, FBC, UEC, LFTs, and pregnancy test
explanation.pearls: Dolutegravir-based PEP has completion rates exceeding 90%, compared with approximately 60% for older raltegravir-based regimens, largely due to once-daily dosing and fewer gastrointestinal side effects.

stem: A 34-year-old woman at 7 weeks gestation following IVF with double embryo transfer presents with acute right iliac fossa pain and light vaginal spotting over the past 6 hours. She has no significant past medical history. Transvaginal ultrasound confirms a viable intrauterine pregnancy with foetal heart activity and a crown-rump length consistent with dates. A 28 mm right adnexal mass with a hyperechoic ring sign is identified, with moderate free fluid in the pouch of Douglas.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 104 bpm, BP 106/68 mmHg, T 36.9 C, RR 18, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Right adnexal tenderness, guarding in right iliac fossa, cervix closed.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 48,000 IU/L, Hb 108 g/L, platelets 245 x10^9/L, group and save held.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Expectant management with serial beta-hCG levels every 48 hours
options.rationale: Expectant management is inappropriate here given haemodynamic compromise (tachycardia, borderline hypotension), free fluid suggesting rupture, and a sizeable adnexal mass. The ectopic component requires definitive surgical treatment. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: Urgent laparoscopic right salpingectomy preserving intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: Heterotopic pregnancy with signs of tubal rupture requires urgent surgical management. Laparoscopic salpingectomy removes the ectopic while preserving the viable intrauterine pregnancy. Methotrexate is absolutely contraindicated due to the coexisting viable IUP. The incidence of heterotopic pregnancy rises to 1-3% with ART. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guidelines; RCOG Green-top Guideline 21)

options.text: Single-dose intramuscular methotrexate at 50 mg per square metre
options.rationale: Methotrexate is absolutely contraindicated in the presence of a viable intrauterine pregnancy as it is teratogenic and embryotoxic. Medical management of ectopic pregnancy is only appropriate when there is no coexisting IUP. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat transvaginal ultrasound in 48 hours to confirm the diagnosis
options.rationale: Delaying intervention with a repeat scan is unsafe in a patient showing signs of haemoperitoneum with tachycardia and free fluid. The ultrasound findings already clearly demonstrate a heterotopic pregnancy with probable tubal rupture. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 21)

options.text: Laparotomy with bilateral salpingectomy and pregnancy termination
options.rationale: Bilateral salpingectomy with termination is unnecessarily radical. The goal is to remove the ectopic component while preserving the viable IUP. Laparoscopy is preferred over laparotomy when the patient is haemodynamically stable enough. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Heterotopic pregnancy occurs when intrauterine and ectopic pregnancies coexist. The incidence is approximately 1 in 30,000 in natural conception but rises to 1-3% with assisted reproduction, particularly with multiple embryo transfer. Management is exclusively surgical because methotrexate is contraindicated with a viable IUP. Laparoscopic salpingectomy is the standard approach when the patient is haemodynamically stable.
key_points: Heterotopic pregnancy incidence is 1-3% after IVF with multiple embryo transfer
Methotrexate is absolutely contraindicated with a coexisting viable IUP
Laparoscopic salpingectomy is preferred over laparotomy when feasible
Free fluid and tachycardia suggest tubal rupture requiring urgent intervention
explanation.pearls: After surgical management of a heterotopic pregnancy, the intrauterine pregnancy continues successfully in approximately 65-70% of cases, with outcomes improving when intervention occurs before significant haemoperitoneum develops.

stem: A 29-year-old woman presents to the early pregnancy assessment unit at 6 weeks gestation with mild left-sided pelvic pain and minimal vaginal spotting. She is haemodynamically stable with no peritoneal signs. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus and a 22 mm left adnexal mass without foetal heart activity. There is no significant free fluid. Her serum beta-hCG is 3,200 IU/L. She has no significant past medical history, normal renal and hepatic function, and a normal full blood count.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 78 bpm, BP 118/74 mmHg, T 36.7 C.
data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 3,200 IU/L, Hb 128 g/L, WCC 7.2 x10^9/L, platelets 268 x10^9/L, creatinine 62 micromol/L, ALT 22 U/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Urgent laparoscopic left salpingectomy under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Surgical management is not first-line for an unruptured ectopic pregnancy that meets medical management criteria. Surgery is reserved for haemodynamic instability, contraindications to methotrexate, failed medical management, or patient preference. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: Serial beta-hCG measurements every 48 hours for expectant management
options.rationale: Expectant management is only appropriate when beta-hCG is below 1,500 IU/L and declining. At 3,200 IU/L with an identified adnexal mass, expectant management carries an unacceptable risk of tubal rupture. Active treatment is indicated. (Source: NICE Ectopic Pregnancy and Miscarriage CG154)
source_refs: NICE Ectopic Pregnancy and Miscarriage CG154

options.text: Single-dose intramuscular methotrexate with day 4 and 7 beta-hCG
options.rationale: This patient meets all criteria for medical management with methotrexate: haemodynamically stable, unruptured ectopic less than 35 mm, no foetal heart activity, beta-hCG below 5,000 IU/L, and no contraindications. The single-dose protocol requires beta-hCG measurement on days 4 and 7, with a 15% decline between those days indicating successful treatment. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guidelines; NICE CG154)
source_refs: NICE Ectopic Pregnancy and Miscarriage CG154

options.text: Transvaginal ultrasound-guided aspiration of the ectopic gestational sac
options.rationale: Ultrasound-guided aspiration of a tubal ectopic pregnancy is not a standard management approach and carries significant risks of incomplete treatment and haemorrhage. This technique may have a role in interstitial or cervical ectopics in specialised centres only. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 21)

options.text: Oral mifepristone 200 mg followed by vaginal misoprostol 800 mcg
options.rationale: Mifepristone and misoprostol is the medical management regimen for intrauterine miscarriage or termination, not for ectopic pregnancy. This regimen does not reliably treat ectopic pregnancies and could dangerously delay appropriate management. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Medical management of ectopic pregnancy with single-dose intramuscular methotrexate is appropriate for haemodynamically stable patients with an unruptured ectopic less than 35 mm, no foetal cardiac activity, serum beta-hCG below 5,000 IU/L, and no contraindications to methotrexate. Success rates are approximately 90% when beta-hCG is below 5,000 IU/L. Patients must be counselled about the need for close follow-up and to avoid conception for at least 3 months after treatment.
key_points: Methotrexate criteria: stable, unruptured, no FHR, mass < 35 mm, beta-hCG < 5,000 IU/L
Day 4 to day 7 beta-hCG decline of at least 15% indicates successful treatment
Patients must avoid folate supplements, NSAIDs, and alcohol during treatment
Surgical management is indicated if methotrexate fails or beta-hCG rises
explanation.pearls: The success rate of single-dose methotrexate falls from approximately 90% when beta-hCG is below 5,000 IU/L to around 70% when beta-hCG is between 5,000 and 10,000 IU/L, making the initial level the strongest predictor of treatment success.

sources.label: NICE Ectopic Pregnancy and Miscarriage CG154

stem: A 38-year-old man was diagnosed with HIV two months ago with a CD4 count of 42 cells per microlitre and a viral load of 320,000 copies per mL. He was commenced on tenofovir, emtricitabine, and dolutegravir. At his 6-week review, his viral load has fallen to 800 copies per mL and CD4 has risen to 145 cells per microlitre. He now presents with 5 days of worsening headache, fever to 38.4 degrees C, and mild neck stiffness. CT brain shows ring-enhancing lesions in the left frontal and right parietal lobes with surrounding oedema. His antiretroviral adherence has been confirmed at greater than 95% by pharmacy records.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92 bpm, BP 132/84 mmHg, T 38.4 C, GCS 14 (E4 V4 M6).
data_table.Investigations: CD4 145 cells per microlitre (baseline 42), viral load 800 copies per mL (baseline 320,000), WCC 8.4 x10^9/L, CRP 68 mg/L. Serum toxoplasma IgG positive. CSF opening pressure 22 cmH2O, protein 0.82 g/L, glucose 3.1 mmol/L (serum 5.8).

lead_in: What is the most likely explanation for this clinical deterioration?

options.text: Antiretroviral treatment failure requiring regimen change to a PI-based combination
options.rationale: Treatment failure is excluded by the dramatic viral load decline from 320,000 to 800 copies per mL and CD4 rise from 42 to 145. Greater than 1 log reduction in viral load by 6 weeks confirms effective treatment. True failure would show stable or rising viral load. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: Primary CNS lymphoma as an AIDS-defining malignancy with progressive disease
options.rationale: Primary CNS lymphoma typically presents with a single enhancing lesion and is more common at CD4 counts below 50. While ring enhancement can occur, the clinical context of rapid immune recovery on effective ART and toxoplasma seropositivity makes IRIS-associated toxoplasmosis far more likely. (Source: BMJ Best Practice: HIV)

options.text: Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy due to JC virus reactivation
options.rationale: PML typically presents with non-enhancing white matter lesions without mass effect on MRI, distinct from the ring-enhancing lesions with oedema seen here. PML also typically causes focal neurological deficits without fever. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: Cryptococcal meningitis due to inadequate antifungal prophylaxis at low CD4 count
options.rationale: While cryptococcal meningitis is common at low CD4 counts, the imaging shows ring-enhancing parenchymal lesions rather than the basal meningeal enhancement typical of cryptococcal disease. The positive toxoplasma IgG and pattern of immune recovery favour IRIS-associated toxoplasmosis. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome unmasking cerebral toxoplasmosis
options.rationale: The clinical picture is classic unmasking IRIS: a patient with very low baseline CD4 starts ART, achieves virological suppression with immune recovery, then develops paradoxical clinical deterioration 2-8 weeks into treatment. Ring-enhancing lesions with positive toxoplasma serology in a recovering immune system indicate IRIS-unmasked cerebral toxoplasmosis. IRIS occurs in 10-25% of patients starting ART at CD4 below 50. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines; Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

explanation.summary: IRIS occurs when a recovering immune system mounts an exaggerated inflammatory response against previously subclinical or partially treated opportunistic infections. Unmasking IRIS reveals infections that were present but clinically silent at baseline. Risk factors include very low baseline CD4 count, high baseline viral load, and rapid virological suppression. Management involves continuing ART, treating the underlying opportunistic infection, and adding corticosteroids for severe inflammation.
key_points: IRIS typically occurs 2-8 weeks after ART initiation in patients with baseline CD4 below 100
Unmasking IRIS reveals subclinical infections; paradoxical IRIS worsens known infections
Confirmed by virological response plus new inflammatory presentation
ART should be continued; stopping ART worsens outcomes
explanation.pearls: The IRIS-associated mortality risk is highest with cryptococcal meningitis IRIS (up to 20-30%), while toxoplasma IRIS has a more favourable prognosis when treated with pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine alongside continued ART and short-course dexamethasone.

stem: A 42-year-old man originally from the Philippines has been living in Australia for 8 years. He is being investigated as a close contact of a colleague recently diagnosed with pulmonary tuberculosis. He is asymptomatic with no cough, fevers, night sweats, or weight loss. His chest X-ray is normal. An interferon-gamma release assay is positive. He has no history of prior TB treatment, no immunosuppressive medications, and is not HIV-positive. His renal and hepatic function are normal.

data_table.Investigations: IGRA (QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus): positive. Chest X-ray: no active disease. HIV Ag/Ab: negative. ALT 28 U/L, AST 24 U/L, creatinine 76 micromol/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate treatment for this patient?

options.text: Isoniazid 300 mg daily with pyridoxine 25 mg daily for 9 months
options.rationale: A positive IGRA with a normal chest X-ray and no symptoms confirms latent TB infection. The standard Australian treatment is isoniazid 300 mg daily for 9 months with pyridoxine supplementation to prevent peripheral neuropathy. This regimen reduces progression to active TB by approximately 90%. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: Rifampicin plus isoniazid plus pyrazinamide plus ethambutol for 2 months
options.rationale: RIPE quadruple therapy is the intensive phase regimen for active tuberculosis, not latent TB. Using full active TB treatment for latent infection exposes the patient to unnecessary drug toxicity without additional benefit over isoniazid monotherapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Rifampicin 600 mg daily as monotherapy for a total of 4 months only
options.rationale: Four months of rifampicin monotherapy is an accepted alternative for latent TB, particularly when isoniazid is contraindicated or not tolerated. However, the standard first-line recommendation in Australian guidelines remains isoniazid for 9 months, not rifampicin monotherapy. (Source: NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: No treatment required as the patient is asymptomatic and CXR normal
options.rationale: A positive IGRA in a close contact of active TB confirms latent infection and carries a significant lifetime risk of progression to active disease (5-10%). Treatment of latent TB is strongly recommended in close contacts regardless of symptom status. Observation alone would miss a key prevention opportunity. (Source: NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat the IGRA in 8 weeks to confirm the result before treating
options.rationale: Repeating the IGRA is unnecessary when the initial result is clearly positive in a known close contact. Delaying treatment increases the risk of progression to active disease, particularly in the first two years after exposure. Close contacts with positive IGRA should commence treatment promptly. (Source: CDNA TB Case Contact Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Latent TB infection is diagnosed when a person has immunological evidence of TB sensitisation without clinical, radiological, or microbiological evidence of active disease. Close contacts of active pulmonary TB cases with a positive IGRA should be offered preventive treatment. Isoniazid for 9 months remains the Australian standard, with rifampicin for 4 months as the main alternative. Baseline and monthly liver function monitoring is recommended during treatment.
key_points: IGRA-positive close contacts should be treated for latent TB regardless of symptoms
Isoniazid 300 mg daily for 9 months is first-line in Australia
Pyridoxine 25 mg daily prevents isoniazid-associated peripheral neuropathy
Monthly LFT monitoring is recommended during isoniazid therapy
explanation.pearls: The 3-month isoniazid plus rifapentine weekly regimen (3HP) used in some countries is not widely available in Australia; the standard remains 9 months of daily isoniazid or 4 months of daily rifampicin as alternatives.

stem: A 36-year-old G3P2 woman at 37 weeks gestation presents with 4 days of progressive dyspnoea, now orthopnoeic with a dry cough. She was seen 48 hours ago by her GP who attributed the symptoms to the late third trimester. She has no history of hypertension or cardiac disease. Her two prior pregnancies were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries. On arrival she is sitting upright, visibly distressed, and unable to complete sentences. Auscultation reveals bilateral basal crackles to the mid-zones and a third heart sound. There is no peripheral oedema beyond mild ankle swelling. Her blood pressure is 124/78 mmHg and urinalysis shows no proteinuria. Bedside echocardiography demonstrates a left ventricular ejection fraction of 28% with global hypokinesis and no valvular pathology.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 118 bpm, BP 124/78 mmHg, RR 32, SpO2 88% on room air, T 36.6 C.
data_table.Investigations: NT-proBNP 8,400 pg/mL, troponin I 0.08 ng/mL (ULN 0.04), Hb 122 g/L, platelets 234 x10^9/L, ALT 32 U/L, creatinine 72 micromol/L, urine PCR < 10 mg/mmol. CTG: reassuring with no decelerations.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management priority?

options.text: Commence IV magnesium sulfate infusion and plan urgent caesarean delivery
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate is indicated for seizure prophylaxis in pre-eclampsia or eclampsia. This patient has no hypertension, no proteinuria, normal platelets, and normal LFTs, making pre-eclampsia unlikely. The echo findings of LVEF 28% point to a primary cardiac pathology. Magnesium could worsen cardiac output. (Source: SOMANZ Guidelines; NHFA Heart Failure Guidelines)

options.text: Administer IV labetalol 20 mg for blood pressure control and monitor closely
options.rationale: Labetalol is appropriate for hypertensive emergencies in pregnancy, but this patient's blood pressure is 124/78 mmHg and does not require antihypertensive therapy. Beta-blockers must also be used cautiously in acute decompensated heart failure as they can worsen cardiogenic shock. (Source: NHFA Heart Failure Guidelines)

options.text: Commence high-flow oxygen and arrange urgent induction of labour with oxytocin
options.rationale: While delivery is ultimately beneficial in peripartum cardiomyopathy, this patient requires immediate cardiac stabilisation before any delivery planning. Oxytocin also has antidiuretic effects and can cause fluid retention, which could worsen pulmonary oedema in a patient with severely reduced LVEF. (Source: NHFA Heart Failure Guidelines)

options.text: Commence IV furosemide and oxygen with urgent cardiology and obstetric review
options.rationale: This patient has peripartum cardiomyopathy presenting with acute decompensated heart failure. The immediate priority is haemodynamic stabilisation with IV diuresis for pulmonary oedema, supplemental oxygen for hypoxia, and urgent joint cardiology-obstetric review. LVEF of 28% with elevated NT-proBNP, third heart sound, and bilateral crackles confirm cardiogenic pulmonary oedema. Delivery timing is a joint decision after stabilisation. (Source: NHFA Heart Failure Guidelines; ESC Peripartum Cardiomyopathy Position Statement)

options.text: Urgent CT pulmonary angiogram to exclude massive pulmonary embolism first
options.rationale: While PE is on the differential for peripartum dyspnoea, the bedside echo showing LVEF 28% with global hypokinesis and a third heart sound already identifies the diagnosis. CTPA would delay treatment, expose mother and foetus to radiation unnecessarily, and the echo findings are not consistent with right heart strain from PE. (Source: NHFA Heart Failure Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Peripartum cardiomyopathy is a diagnosis of exclusion defined as heart failure with LVEF below 45% developing in the last month of pregnancy or within 5 months postpartum. It can mimic pre-eclampsia with dyspnoea and oedema, but the absence of hypertension, proteinuria, and hepatic or haematological derangement distinguishes the two. Immediate management focuses on stabilising heart failure before delivery planning. Long-term prognosis depends on LVEF recovery, with approximately 50% of patients recovering normal function within 6 months.
key_points: PPCM is defined as LVEF < 45% in late pregnancy or early postpartum with no prior cardiac disease
Normal BP and absent proteinuria distinguish PPCM from pre-eclampsia with pulmonary oedema
IV furosemide and oxygen are first-line for acute cardiogenic pulmonary oedema in pregnancy
Delivery timing requires joint cardiology-obstetric decision after haemodynamic stabilisation
explanation.pearls: Bromocriptine 2.5 mg twice daily for 2 weeks then 2.5 mg daily for 6 weeks has shown promise in improving LVEF recovery in peripartum cardiomyopathy by blocking the pro-apoptotic 16 kDa prolactin fragment, though it requires cessation of breastfeeding.

subtopic_detail: Antiphospholipid syndrome in recurrent pregnancy loss
stem: A 31-year-old woman attends the recurrent miscarriage clinic after three consecutive first-trimester losses, all between 8 and 10 weeks gestation. She has no living children. Investigations reveal persistently positive lupus anticoagulant on two occasions 14 weeks apart and anticardiolipin IgG at 62 GPL units (positive > 40). Her partner's karyotype and her own are normal. Thyroid function, glucose tolerance, and uterine cavity assessment by saline infusion sonography are all normal. She is now 6 weeks pregnant again, confirmed by ultrasound showing an intrauterine gestational sac with a yolk sac.

data_table.Investigations: Lupus anticoagulant: positive (confirmed on two occasions 14 weeks apart). Anticardiolipin IgG: 62 GPL (positive > 40). Anti-beta2 glycoprotein I IgG: negative. FBC, UEC, LFTs, TFTs: normal. OGTT: normal. Parental karyotypes: normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate treatment for this pregnancy?

options.text: Low-dose aspirin 150 mg daily alone without any anticoagulation therapy
options.rationale: Aspirin alone is insufficient for obstetric antiphospholipid syndrome with recurrent pregnancy loss. The combination of aspirin plus heparin has been shown to significantly improve live birth rates compared with aspirin alone in APS-associated recurrent miscarriage. (Source: RANZCOG APS in Pregnancy; RCOG Green-top Guideline 17)

options.text: Therapeutic-dose enoxaparin 1.5 mg per kg daily without additional aspirin
options.rationale: Therapeutic-dose anticoagulation is indicated for APS with prior thrombotic events, not for purely obstetric APS without thrombotic history. Prophylactic-dose enoxaparin combined with aspirin is the standard for obstetric APS. Therapeutic dosing increases bleeding risk without proven additional benefit for pregnancy outcomes. (Source: RANZCOG APS in Pregnancy)

options.text: Low-dose aspirin 150 mg daily plus prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg daily
options.rationale: The standard of care for obstetric APS with recurrent pregnancy loss is the combination of low-dose aspirin (commenced preconception or at positive pregnancy test) plus prophylactic-dose low-molecular-weight heparin (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg daily) commenced once a viable intrauterine pregnancy is confirmed. This combination improves live birth rates from approximately 40% to 70-80%. (Source: RANZCOG APS in Pregnancy; RCOG Green-top Guideline 17)

options.text: Warfarin at a target INR of 2.0 to 3.0 throughout the entire pregnancy
options.rationale: Warfarin is teratogenic in the first trimester (causing warfarin embryopathy between 6 and 12 weeks) and is contraindicated in the first trimester and near term. It is replaced by LMWH in pregnancy. Warfarin may only be considered mid-trimester in women with mechanical heart valves. (Source: RANZCOG APS in Pregnancy)

options.text: Prednisolone 20 mg daily plus low-dose aspirin throughout the pregnancy
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are not recommended for obstetric APS. Trials showed no benefit for pregnancy outcomes and increased maternal morbidity including gestational diabetes, hypertension, and preterm birth. The combination of aspirin plus heparin has superior evidence. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 17)

explanation.summary: Antiphospholipid syndrome is diagnosed when at least one clinical criterion (vascular thrombosis or pregnancy morbidity) and at least one laboratory criterion (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, or anti-beta2 glycoprotein I antibodies confirmed on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart) are met. In obstetric APS with recurrent pregnancy loss, aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH is the standard treatment, improving live birth rates substantially. Close antenatal surveillance including serial growth scans and uterine artery Doppler is recommended due to increased risk of pre-eclampsia and placental insufficiency.
key_points: Diagnosis requires clinical plus laboratory criteria with antibodies confirmed 12 weeks apart
Aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH is standard for obstetric APS without prior thrombosis
Warfarin is teratogenic in the first trimester and contraindicated
These pregnancies require surveillance for pre-eclampsia, FGR, and placental abruption
explanation.pearls: Triple-positive APS (positive lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, and anti-beta2 glycoprotein I) carries the highest thrombotic risk and worst obstetric outcomes, with some experts advocating for higher-intensity anticoagulation in this subgroup despite limited trial evidence.

stem: A 26-year-old G2P1 woman at 9 weeks gestation presents with heavy vaginal bleeding and cramping lower abdominal pain for 6 hours. She has soaked through four pads in the last 2 hours. She is Rh-negative. On examination, the cervical os is open with products of conception visible at the os. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an intrauterine gestational sac containing a foetal pole with no cardiac activity and a crown-rump length of 18 mm. She is haemodynamically stable and wishes active management to resolve the miscarriage quickly.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 94 bpm, BP 112/72 mmHg, T 36.8 C.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 116 g/L, blood group A Rh-negative, no irregular antibodies detected.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Oral mifepristone 200 mg followed by misoprostol 800 mcg in 24 to 48 hours
options.rationale: Mifepristone-misoprostol is the medical management regimen for missed miscarriage when the cervix is closed. In inevitable miscarriage with an open os and products visible, surgical evacuation is more appropriate, particularly with heavy ongoing bleeding. Medical management risks prolonged bleeding. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss Guidelines)

options.text: Surgical evacuation of retained products plus anti-D immunoglobulin 250 IU
options.rationale: This patient has an inevitable miscarriage (open os, products visible, no cardiac activity) with heavy bleeding and a preference for rapid resolution. Surgical evacuation is the most appropriate active management. As she is Rh-negative and beyond 10 weeks by CRL sizing, anti-D immunoglobulin is required to prevent Rh isoimmunisation. At less than 12 weeks, the dose is 250 IU. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss Guidelines; ANZSBT Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Expectant management with review in one week and serial beta-hCG levels
options.rationale: While expectant management is an option for some miscarriages, this patient has heavy bleeding with products at the os and has expressed a preference for active management. Expectant management would prolong her symptoms and carries a risk of incomplete miscarriage and infection. (Source: NICE NG126: Ectopic Pregnancy and Miscarriage)
source_refs: NICE NG126: Ectopic Pregnancy and Miscarriage

options.text: Vaginal misoprostol 800 mcg alone without prior mifepristone as a single dose
options.rationale: While misoprostol alone can be used for medical management of miscarriage, surgical evacuation is preferred when products are already at the os and bleeding is heavy. Medical management alone has a higher incomplete evacuation rate and would not address the patient's preference for rapid resolution. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss Guidelines)

options.text: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV plus oxytocin infusion to reduce bleeding and observe
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid and oxytocin may reduce bleeding symptomatically but do not address the retained products of conception. Definitive management requires either surgical or medical evacuation. Temporising with uterotonics alone risks incomplete miscarriage and sepsis. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Inevitable miscarriage is characterised by an open cervical os with or without products of conception visible, often with heavy bleeding. Management options include surgical evacuation, medical management, or expectant management. Patient preference and clinical circumstances guide the choice. Rh-negative women require anti-D immunoglobulin after any pregnancy loss to prevent isoimmunisation in future pregnancies. The dose depends on gestational age: 250 IU before 12 weeks and 625 IU at or after 12 weeks in Australia.
key_points: Open os with products visible defines inevitable miscarriage
Surgical evacuation is preferred for heavy bleeding and patient preference for rapid resolution
Anti-D 250 IU is required for Rh-negative women losing a pregnancy before 12 weeks
All three management options are valid but clinical context guides selection
explanation.pearls: Anti-D immunoglobulin is not required for threatened miscarriage before 12 weeks unless bleeding is heavy, recurrent, or associated with abdominal pain, but it IS required for all complete, incomplete, and inevitable miscarriages regardless of gestational age in Rh-negative women.

sources.label: NICE NG126: Ectopic Pregnancy and Miscarriage

subtopic_detail: Menopausal hormone therapy route and VTE risk
stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with severe vasomotor symptoms including 12 to 15 hot flushes daily and significant sleep disturbance for the past 8 months since her last menstrual period. Her FSH is 68 IU/L confirming menopause. She has a BMI of 36 kg per square metre, a personal history of provoked deep vein thrombosis following knee arthroscopy 3 years ago (completed 3 months of rivaroxaban, now off anticoagulation), and well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine. She has an intact uterus. Lifestyle measures have been insufficient. She is requesting hormonal treatment for her symptoms.

data_table.Investigations: FSH 68 IU/L, oestradiol < 100 pmol/L, TSH 2.4 mIU/L, FBC normal, LFTs normal, lipid profile: total cholesterol 5.8 mmol/L, LDL 3.4 mmol/L, HDL 1.2 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate hormonal treatment approach?

options.text: Oral conjugated equine oestrogen 0.625 mg daily plus oral medroxyprogesterone
options.rationale: Oral oestrogen undergoes first-pass hepatic metabolism, which increases hepatic synthesis of clotting factors and raises VTE risk 2-4 fold. In a woman with prior VTE and BMI over 35, oral oestrogen is relatively contraindicated due to compounding thrombotic risk factors. (Source: AMS Position Statement on MHT)

options.text: Oral tibolone 2.5 mg daily as a combined oestrogenic and progestogenic agent
options.rationale: Tibolone, while having fewer oestrogenic effects on coagulation than conjugated oestrogens, is still an oral agent with first-pass metabolism. Its VTE risk profile in women with prior thrombotic events is not sufficiently reassuring compared to transdermal oestradiol, which avoids hepatic first-pass entirely. (Source: AMS Position Statement on MHT)

options.text: Venlafaxine 75 mg daily as a non-hormonal alternative for vasomotor symptoms
options.rationale: Non-hormonal options such as venlafaxine can reduce hot flush frequency by approximately 50-60%, but this patient is specifically requesting hormonal treatment and her symptoms are severe. A safer hormonal route exists that avoids the VTE risk of oral oestrogen. Non-hormonal therapy should be reserved for absolute contraindications. (Source: AMS Position Statement on MHT)

options.text: Oral oestradiol valerate 2 mg daily plus micronised progesterone 100 mg daily
options.rationale: Although micronised progesterone is the preferred progestogen for metabolic safety, the oral oestradiol component still carries the first-pass hepatic VTE risk. In a woman with prior VTE and obesity, the oestrogen route must be transdermal rather than oral, regardless of which progestogen is chosen. (Source: AMS Position Statement on MHT)

options.text: Transdermal oestradiol 50 mcg patch plus oral micronised progesterone nightly
options.rationale: Transdermal oestradiol bypasses hepatic first-pass metabolism and does not increase VTE risk, making it the preferred route in women with prior VTE or elevated thrombotic risk. Observational data consistently show no increased VTE risk with transdermal oestradiol at standard doses. Micronised progesterone is the preferred progestogen as it has the most favourable safety profile for VTE, breast, and cardiovascular outcomes. (Source: AMS Position Statement on MHT; Lancet 2019 meta-analysis)

explanation.summary: Menopausal hormone therapy route selection is critical in women with VTE risk factors. Transdermal oestradiol avoids hepatic first-pass metabolism and therefore does not increase clotting factor synthesis or VTE risk. This contrasts with oral oestrogen, which increases VTE risk 2-4 fold through hepatic effects. Micronised progesterone is preferred over synthetic progestogens for its more favourable safety profile. Women with a prior VTE are not absolutely contraindicated from MHT if the transdermal route is used, though shared decision-making with a haematologist may be advisable.
key_points: Transdermal oestradiol avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism and does not increase VTE risk
Oral oestrogen increases VTE risk 2-4 fold via hepatic clotting factor synthesis
Micronised progesterone has the lowest VTE risk among progestogens
Prior provoked VTE is a relative, not absolute, contraindication to transdermal MHT
explanation.pearls: The ESTHER and E3N French cohort studies demonstrated that transdermal oestradiol combined with micronised progesterone carried no measurable increase in VTE risk (OR 0.9, 95% CI 0.5-1.6), even in women with BMI above 30 or thrombophilia.

subtopic_detail: Management of cervical screening abnormality during pregnancy
stem: A 30-year-old G1P0 woman at 14 weeks gestation has her routine cervical screening test result return as HPV 16 positive with a low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion on liquid-based cytology. She had a normal cervical screening test 3 years ago. She is well with no vaginal bleeding or discharge. She has no history of abnormal cervical screening and is a non-smoker.

data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening: HPV 16 detected. LBC: low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (LSIL).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Colposcopy during pregnancy without biopsy unless high-grade disease suspected
options.rationale: HPV 16 positivity with any cytological abnormality requires colposcopy. Colposcopy is safe in pregnancy and should be performed to exclude high-grade disease. Biopsy is reserved for suspected high-grade or invasive lesions only, as routine biopsy risks bleeding from the hypervascular cervix. Treatment is deferred until postpartum. (Source: NCSP Guidelines; RANZCOG Cervical Screening in Pregnancy)

options.text: Defer all investigation until 6 weeks postpartum for repeat cervical screen
options.rationale: HPV 16 is the highest-risk HPV genotype and deferring all investigation until postpartum risks missing an underlying high-grade lesion or early invasive carcinoma. Colposcopy should be performed during pregnancy to exclude serious pathology, even though treatment is deferred. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

options.text: Immediate large loop excision of the transformation zone under local anaesthesia
options.rationale: LLETZ is contraindicated during pregnancy due to the risk of haemorrhage from the hypervascular pregnant cervix, cervical incompetence, preterm rupture of membranes, and pregnancy loss. Excisional treatment is deferred to at least 3 months postpartum. (Source: RANZCOG Cervical Screening in Pregnancy)

options.text: Repeat cervical screening in 12 months as HPV 16 with LSIL is low risk
options.rationale: HPV 16 with any cytological abnormality is not low risk. HPV 16 has the highest oncogenic potential of all HPV genotypes and requires direct colposcopic assessment regardless of the cytology grade. A 12-month repeat would be inappropriate for this high-risk genotype. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

options.text: Cervical biopsy under ultrasound guidance to obtain histological confirmation
options.rationale: Ultrasound-guided cervical biopsy is not a standard technique for cervical screening follow-up. Colposcopy is the standard assessment method. Biopsy during pregnancy is performed only under colposcopic guidance when a high-grade or invasive lesion is suspected. (Source: NCSP Guidelines)

explanation.summary: The Australian National Cervical Screening Program recommends colposcopy for any HPV 16 or 18 detection regardless of cytology result. Colposcopy is safe during pregnancy and should be performed to exclude invasive disease. Routine biopsies are avoided due to bleeding risk from cervical hypervascularity, but directed biopsies are taken if high-grade or invasive disease is suspected. Treatment with excisional procedures is deferred until at least 3 months postpartum, with repeat colposcopy recommended at that time.
key_points: HPV 16 or 18 detection mandates colposcopy regardless of cytology grade
Colposcopy is safe in pregnancy; treatment is deferred postpartum
Biopsy only if high-grade or invasive disease is colposcopically suspected
LLETZ is contraindicated during pregnancy due to haemorrhage and cervical incompetence risk
explanation.pearls: HPV 16 is responsible for approximately 55-60% of all cervical cancers and has a 12% 10-year risk of CIN3+ even when the initial cytology is normal, which is why the NCSP mandates colposcopy for HPV 16 regardless of cytology.
